1.synthesis:
The Ziphites betray David again, and Saul marches to the wilderness of Ziph with 3,000 chosen men (v. 2). David scouts the camp, observes Saul sleeping in the camp with Abner beside him and the army around him, and asks his men: who will go down into the camp with me? (v. 6). Abishai volunteers; together they enter the camp at night. Saul is asleep; his spear is stuck in the ground at his head (v. 7). Abishai urges a single strike: “God has given your enemy into your hand this day. Now please let me pin him to the earth with one stroke of the spear, and I will not strike him twice” (v. 8). David refuses: “Do not destroy him, for who can put out his hand against the LORD’s anointed and be guiltless?... The LORD will strike him, or his day will come to die, or he will go down into battle and perish. The LORD forbid that I should put out my hand against the LORD’s anointed” (vv. 9–11). David takes the spear and the water jar from beside Saul’s head and they depart.

The repetition of the En-gedi encounter with significant variations makes the theological point doubly clear: David’s restraint is not situational luck but a principled commitment. This time the opportunity is even more direct — Saul is asleep and helpless — and David has a companion urging him to act. He refuses again and articulates the principle more fully: three possible fates for Saul are named (YHWH strikes him, he dies naturally, he dies in battle), and all three are YHWH’s domain. YHWH’s deep sleep on the entire camp (v. 12) confirms the providential dimension: the mission was enabled by YHWH, and the restraint was David’s within YHWH’s enabling.
1.historical_context: The spear stuck in the ground at the sleeping king’s head is a symbol of royal authority — the king’s spear was a marker of his presence and power. Taking it along with the water jar (life-sustaining provision) removes the symbols of Saul’s royal status and personal survival from beside his defenseless head — a symbolic stripping that demonstrates access without execution. YHWH’s “deep sleep” (tardemah) on the camp is the same word used for Adam’s deep sleep in Genesis 2:21 and Abraham’s in Genesis 15:12 — a divinely imposed state of unconsciousness that enables something significant to happen.
1.literary_structure: The second Saul-sparing account (26:1–12) parallels the En-gedi account (ch. 24) with deliberate variations: companions (one in ch. 26 vs. none in ch. 24), weapon taken (spear and water jar vs. robe-corner), and the explicit articulation of the three fates YHWH may choose for Saul. The doubling confirms that David’s restraint is a principle, not an accident. The spear — Saul’s weapon, which has been thrown at David, Jonathan, and the priests — is now peacefully in David’s hand rather than Saul’s, a visual embodiment of the reversal that has already occurred in the divine ordering even if not yet in the political reality.
1.theological_application: The three alternatives David names for Saul’s end — divine judgment, natural death, death in battle — represent the full range of YHWH’s means for executing his purposes in history. David’s refusal to use any of them is not passivity but active submission to YHWH’s sovereignty over the timing and means of historical transition. The posture of “I will not put my hand against the LORD’s anointed” requires believing that YHWH is actively managing the situation and that the outcome is not dependent on David’s willingness to act. Where Abishai sees a providential opportunity, David sees a boundary. Both are reading the same situation; their difference is in what they believe YHWH’s sovereignty requires of them in that moment.
1.christology: David’s holding Saul’s spear — the weapon that had been thrown at him, at Jonathan, that had become the instrument of Saul’s paranoia — and choosing not to use it is the type of the one who could have called twelve legions of angels (Matt 26:53) and chose not to. The Son of David had at his disposal every means of divine judgment against those who arrested, tried, and crucified him; he chose to entrust himself to the one who judges justly. The spear David held and then replaced beside the sleeping king prefigures the nails and the cross: instruments that could have been refused, held by the anointed one in restraint, so that YHWH’s greater purpose could be accomplished.

13.synthesis:
David crosses to the other side and stands on the top of a hill with a large distance between them — safety through elevation and distance — and calls out to the army and to Abner (vv. 13–14). He mocks Abner: “Are you not a man? Who is your equal in Israel? Why then have you not kept watch over your lord the king? For one of the people came in to destroy the king your lord... You have not kept your lord, the LORD’s anointed. And now see where the king’s spear is and the jar of water that was at his head” (vv. 15–16). Saul recognizes David’s voice. David makes the same appeal as at En-gedi: what have I done? Why is the king pursuing me? — and names YHWH as the one who can adjudicate.

Saul’s response, like En-gedi, is a moment of clarity and confession: “I have sinned. Return, my son David, for I will no more do you harm, because my life was precious in your eyes this day. Behold, I have acted foolishly, and have made a great mistake” (v. 21). David returns the spear but does not return to Saul; he asks YHWH to reward his righteousness. Their exchange closes with Saul blessing David and prophesying: “You will do many things and will succeed in them” (v. 25). They separate; David goes on his way and Saul returns home. The two will not meet again.
13.historical_context: David’s public rebuke of Abner — the commander of Saul’s army and his cousin (1 Sam 14:50) — is both a shaming and a demonstration: the army failed to protect the king, and the fugitive could have killed him. The rebuke serves David’s purpose of demonstrating that he is not a threat to Saul’s life while simultaneously showing that Saul’s military apparatus has failed. Saul’s return of the spear — “let one of the young men come over and take it” — and David’s response of “here is the spear, O king; let one of the young men come over and take it” enacts a formal return of royal symbols from a safe distance.
13.literary_structure: The second Saul-encounter encounter (26:13–25) closes with a more complete symmetry than En-gedi: Saul not only acknowledges David’s righteousness but explicitly confesses sin and blesses David’s future. The verbal exchange — rebuke, confession, appeal, blessing, prophecy — is the most complete acknowledgment of David’s position Saul has ever made. Yet it leads to the same outcome: separation. Acknowledgment without structural change is insufficient — Saul cannot reorganize his reign around its implications. The final separation is both the end of the Saul-David direct encounters and the implicit close of Saul’s chances for genuine repentance.
13.theological_application: Saul’s confession “I have sinned... I have acted foolishly” (v. 21) is linguistically a genuine confession but functionally insufficient — it does not change the structural situation that produced the pursuit. The difference between acknowledgment and repentance is the willingness to restructure one’s life around the implications of what has been confessed. Saul confesses twice (here and at En-gedi) and pursues David again after each confession — the pattern of repeated confession without behavioral change that the OT consistently diagnoses as hardness of heart that cannot hear what the mouth is saying. Communities of faith must attend to whether confession produces structural change or merely emotional release.
13.christology: Saul’s final blessing of David — “You will do many things and will succeed in them” (v. 25) — is an unwilling prophecy from the rejected king to the anointed one he cannot stop: the ultimate acknowledgment that what YHWH has purposed will come to pass. It echoes Caiaphas’s unwilling prophecy in John 11:51: “he did not say this of his own accord, but being high priest that year he prophesied.” Both the rejected king of Israel and the high priest of Jerusalem speak truth about the anointed one without intending to; both are witnesses against their own opposition. YHWH’s purpose is confirmed by the mouths of those who most want to resist it.
